title: Gestgo — the 90-second 'what to eat' companion
subtitle: Positioning doc for the food-discovery side project. Current status: backend runs, no real frontend yet. This is the 'elevator pitch you wish you had' one-pager for conversations with potential collaborators or investors.
meta: review needed: voice, naming (Gestgo vs Gusto), scope

blocks.label: The 5-second pitch
blocks.text: Gestgo helps you decide what to eat in under 90 seconds — by behaving like a friend who already knows your taste, not a restaurant search engine.

blocks.markdown:
## The problem

Choosing what to eat at a new restaurant is a small but recurring decision fatigue. For international students and young professionals, the standard tools (Yelp, Google Maps, Meituan) all do the same thing: dump 200 options and make *you* decide. By the time you've decoded three menus, the urge is gone.

The friction isn't information access. It's *decision support*.

## The insight

The best food experiences are already curated — by a friend who knows you. 'Get the tonkotsu here, you'll love it.' That's one sentence, zero decisions.

Gestgo is trying to be that friend at scale.

## What it does

1. **Onboards in under 2 minutes** via a visual taste test (swipe 10 food photos, LLM generates a taste profile)
2. **At decision time**, asks *one* contextual question ('craving something light or heavy?') and shows *one* recommendation card
3. **Remembers** every pick + skip + feedback moment as part of your persistent taste graph
4. **Grows more confident over time** — after 5 outings, it doesn't need to ask; it just suggests

## Why now

- LLMs can generate context-aware menu interpretations without needing proprietary restaurant data
- Mobile camera + vision models turn a menu photo into structured data in <3s
- Users are increasingly burned out on 'here are 200 options' apps (TikTok's food discovery has conditioned them to expect *curation*)

## What makes it different

| Existing tool | What they do | What they don't do |
|---|---|---|
| Yelp / Google Maps | Show every option, reviews, photos | Never decide for you |
| Tabelog / Meituan | Regional expertise + reviews | Same problem, denser |
| Tinder-for-restaurants apps | Let you swipe through cards | Don't remember why you picked; no follow-through |
| **Gestgo** | One card at a time; context-aware; accumulating taste model | *(this is the bet)* |

**The UX thesis — 'Orb Hub + 3 Moments':**

- **Orb** is the single visual anchor — a breathing circle that represents Gestgo. Conversation mode (orb center, talking) + Display mode (orb shrinks to top-dot, content takes over).
- **3 moments** per outing: *before* (what should I get), *during* (the story of this restaurant), *after* (how was it).
- Every session starts and ends at Orb Home — a hub with floating memory bubbles of past meals.

This isn't a restaurant search app. It's a *companion pattern*.

## Status today (2026-04-22)

- **Backend:** FastAPI + OpenAI, working MVP for recommendation + dish info
- **Frontend:** A 2476-line single HTML file — visual prototype, not productionized
- **Phase 2 plan:** Next.js PWA + Supabase + Claude API, full Orb UX
- **Dataset:** Google Places + LLM + Web Search for dish data (better than any menu API because it's real-time)

## What a v1 launch would prove

1. Users can complete onboarding (<2 min) without dropping off — **retention target: 70%**
2. The 'one card at a time' UX feels *better* than swiping 20 restaurants — **qualitative + time-to-decision metric**
3. The second-session experience is faster than the first — **proves the taste model actually learns**

## Moat candidates

| Moat | Strength | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Taste model depth** (per user) | Strong | Every interaction strengthens it; migration cost grows over time |
| **Orb UX pattern** | Moderate | Distinctive but copyable by anyone willing to ship it |
| **Curation taste** (brand voice) | Strong if nailed | Hard to replicate; this is where the founder's personal taste becomes a feature |
| **Data flywheel** | Weak at current scale | Needs 10k+ active users before network effects materialize |

**Working thesis:** the taste model + founder curation voice is the real moat. The Orb UX is the *discovery* surface that makes the taste model collectable in the first place.

## The 3 biggest open questions

1. **Naming.** Gusto has stronger phonetic + gustatory association; 'Gestgo' reads as a contraction. **Decision needed:** pick one, kill the other.
2. **Platform.** PWA (ships fast, shareable URL) vs native (better camera + haptics for Orb). Current plan says PWA; worth re-examining.
3. **Monetization.** Free with affiliate links to reservations? Subscription? Restaurant partnerships? Not blocking v1 but shapes v2 architecture.

## What I'd ship next (in priority order)

1. **Name & brand decision** (1 day) — lock Gusto vs Gestgo before writing more code
2. **Next.js + Supabase scaffold** with Orb Home as the only screen (~1 week)
3. **Visual taste test onboarding** (Moment 0, ~3 days)
4. **Before Moment** (one restaurant card + one dish card) — the 'what to eat' core loop (~1 week)
5. **Ten friendly users testing the full before-during-after loop over 2 weeks** — structured feedback
6. **Iterate or pivot** based on whether the 'second outing is faster' hypothesis holds

Total: ~4-6 weeks to answer whether this is worth continuing.

## Related side-project alignment

Gestgo + the self-evolving-persona research (see `/experiments/self-evolving-persona`) share the same core primitive: **a durable user model that evolves from behavior, not questionnaires.** A shared personal-context layer across all of Wenxuan's apps would be the biggest compounding bet — Crumbs knows your MBTI, BedRock knows your work, Gestgo knows your taste. They could all feed one persona core.

blocks.label: Decision I need from Wenxuan
blocks.text: This one-pager is a draft — voice/tone/accuracy are my best guesses based on the docs dump. Please read it with a red pen: what's off, what's missing, what's misattributed. Once it's accurate, we can use it as the canonical pitch for conversations.

items: Pick monetization thesis for roadmap framing (not v1 blocker)
Draft a 60-second demo video script once v1 ships
Capture founder-voice examples (Wenxuan's actual food recommendations to friends) as training data for the brand voice
